transactions-treasury.title: Intents API transaction signing
transactions-treasury.summary: Let an agent sign and submit an on-chain transaction while the private key stays in the vault.

blocks.text: The Intents API lets an agent sign transactions without ever seeing the raw private key. The vault decrypts the key inside the HSM, signs, and broadcasts. The agent only gets back a tx_hash.

items: POST /v1/agents/:id/transactions signs and broadcasts
POST /v1/agents/:id/transactions/sign signs only (BYORPC), returns signed_tx
Unified POST /v1/agents/:id/sign takes intent_type: personal_sign, typed_data, or transaction

blocks.text: The intents_api_enabled flag is two-sided. It grants the transaction endpoints and also blocks direct reads of private_key or ssh_key through the normal secrets endpoint, so the agent can only use keys through the proxy.

steps.text: Get a human API key from the dashboard at Settings, API Keys.
Create an agent with intents_api_enabled and guardrails so runaway spend is capped.
steps.code:
curl -X POST "https://api.1claw.xyz/v1/agents" \
  -H "Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN" -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{
    "name": "DeFi Bot",
    "intents_api_enabled": true,
    "tx_allowed_chains": ["sepolia", "base"],
    "tx_to_allowlist": ["0xRecipient"],
    "tx_max_value_eth": "0.01",
    "tx_daily_limit_eth": "0.05"
  }'

steps.text: Provision an HSM-backed Ethereum signing key for the agent. The private key lands in the __agent-keys vault.
Exchange the agent api_key for a JWT.
Submit a transaction on Sepolia with simulate_first so a revert returns 422 and nothing is signed.

blocks.text: Want to sign but broadcast yourself? Call /transactions/sign with the same body. You get signed_tx and tx_hash back, and all guardrails still apply.
You now have an agent that submits a simulated, guardrailed transaction with the signing key locked inside the vault.

quiz.question: What does intents_api_enabled: true do besides granting the transaction endpoints?
options: It exports the private key to the agent
It blocks the agent from reading private_key or ssh_key via the secrets endpoint
It rotates the signing key nightly
quiz.explanation: The flag is two-sided: it grants transaction endpoints and blocks direct signing-key reads so keys are only usable through the proxy.

quiz.question: What happens when simulate_first: true and the simulation reverts?
options: The API returns 422 and does not sign or broadcast
quiz.explanation: On a reverted simulation the API returns 422 and never signs or broadcasts.

quiz.question: Which endpoint signs a transaction without broadcasting it?
quiz.explanation: The /transactions/sign endpoint returns signed_tx for you to broadcast yourself, with guardrails still enforced.

transactions-treasury.title: Treasury wallets and smart accounts
transactions-treasury.summary: Create human-owned multi-chain treasury wallets and drive a Safe multisig with proposals.

blocks.text: Treasury wallets are native HSM-backed wallets for human users across six chains. Keys are generated server-side, stored in a per-org __treasury-keys vault, and never leave the enclave unless you export them.

Treasury wallets require a Pro or higher plan and enforce require_human. Agents calling any treasury wallet endpoint get a 403. Direct API reads of __treasury-keys also return 403; use the export endpoint instead.

items: Six chains: Ethereum, Bitcoin, Solana, XRP, Cardano, Tron
Custody is XOR 2-of-2 on Pro and Team, Shamir 2-of-3 on Business and Enterprise
Safe multisig proposals collect signatures and auto-execute at threshold
Agent delegation modes: Owner (agent EOA is a Safe signer) or Delegated (agent signs with the treasury key via Intents API)

steps.text: Use a human API key. Free tier gets a 403 here.
Generate treasury wallets. Omit chains to create all six.
Create a treasury bound to a Safe address so you can manage signers and proposals.
Create a multisig proposal against that treasury.
Sign the proposal. When approve signatures reach the Safe threshold, the proposal auto-executes and broadcasts.

blocks.text: In Delegated mode the agent submits transactions through the Intents API with treasury_id and mode: treasury. The handler applies the strictest of the agent-level and delegation-level guardrails.
You now have a treasury wallet and a Safe proposal that collects signatures and executes on its own once threshold is met.

quiz.question: Why does an agent get a 403 when calling a treasury wallet endpoint?
options: The agent has no signing key
quiz.explanation: All treasury wallet endpoints enforce require_human, so agents receive a 403.

quiz.question: In Delegated mode, where does the treasury private key live when an agent signs?
options: It is copied to the agent
It stays in __treasury-keys and signs via the Intents API
It is returned in the response
It moves to the Safe contract
quiz.explanation: Delegated mode signs with the treasury key through the Intents API; the key never leaves __treasury-keys.

quiz.question: What triggers a Safe proposal to execute automatically?
options: Approve signatures reaching the Safe threshold
quiz.explanation: When approve signatures reach the Safe threshold, the proposal auto-executes and broadcasts.

transactions-treasury.title: x402 micropayments for overages
transactions-treasury.summary: Handle an HTTP 402 from the API and retry with an on-chain payment proof.

blocks.text: When your org is over its monthly request quota and the overage method is x402, payable endpoints return 402 Payment Required. You pay on Base with USDC and retry with an X-PAYMENT header.

items: 402 body is spec-compliant with an accepts[] array
accepts[] uses scheme exact, network eip155:8453, asset USDC on Base
Amounts are atomic units (USDC has 6 decimals, so 1500 is 0.0015 USDC)
1Claw settles through the Coinbase CDP facilitator on Base (chain 8453)

blocks.text: New orgs default to credits for overages. Switch to x402 explicitly before you rely on per-request on-chain payment.

steps.text: Set your credentials.
Switch the org overage method to x402.
Hit a payable endpoint. When over quota you get a 402 with accepts[].
Read the 402 accepts[] entry: scheme, network, maxAmountRequired, payTo, and asset.
Generate an EOA key, fund it with USDC on Base, and let an x402 client sign the payment and retry. The x402-payments example does this with @x402/evm.
steps.code:
cd examples/x402-payments
npm install && cp .env.example .env
# set X402_PRIVATE_KEY, ONECLAW_API_KEY, ONECLAW_VAULT_ID
npm start

blocks.text: Run npm run probe with no payment key to confirm the 402 behavior before you fund a wallet. It only sends GET requests and prints status codes.
You can now take a 402, sign a USDC payment on Base, and retry the same request with X-PAYMENT to get a 200.

quiz.question: Which network and asset do 1Claw x402 payments use?
quiz.explanation: The accepts[] entry uses network eip155:8453 with USDC on Base, settled via the Coinbase CDP facilitator.

quiz.question: The 402 shows maxAmountRequired: 1500 for USDC. What is that?
quiz.explanation: Amounts are atomic units and USDC has 6 decimals, so 1500 is 0.0015 USDC.

quiz.question: After the API returns 402, how does the client complete the request?
options: It waits for the quota to reset
It signs the payment and retries with an X-PAYMENT header
quiz.explanation: An x402 client pays the required amount on-chain and retries with the X-PAYMENT header.

transactions-treasury.title: Embedded wallets and wallet-react
transactions-treasury.summary: Give users an email-login wallet and render it with the wallet-react widget.

blocks.text: Embedded wallets give users a wallet from just an email address. No extensions, no seed phrases. You provision them with a platform app key and email OTP login.
You need a Pro or higher plan and a platform app plt_ key created in the dashboard at the Platform page.

steps.text: Install the SDK.
Create the client with your platform app key.
steps.code:
import { createClient } from "@1claw/sdk";
const client = createClient({ baseUrl: "https://api.1claw.xyz", apiKey: "plt_..." });

steps.text: Send a login code to the user's email.
Verify the 6-digit code and auto-provision an Ethereum wallet. You get back a token and wallet_address.
Set a spend policy so users cannot move more than you allow.
Render the widget with wallet-react. OneclawWalletProvider wraps OneclawEmbeddedWallet.
Drop the provider and widget into your app.

blocks.text: You can override the app-wide spend policy per user with client.platform.setUserSpendPolicy(connectionId, {...}).
You now render a working embedded wallet widget backed by email OTP login and a spend policy.

quiz.question: What do you need before creating embedded wallets?
options: A Free plan and a 1ck_ key
A Pro+ plan and a platform app plt_ key
quiz.explanation: Embedded wallets require a Pro or higher plan plus a platform app plt_ key.

quiz.question: How does a user log in to get a wallet?
options: Email OTP with sendEmailOtp and verifyEmailOtp
quiz.explanation: You send a code with sendEmailOtp and confirm it with verifyEmailOtp, which returns the wallet_address.

quiz.question: Which two components does wallet-react provide for the widget?
quiz.explanation: OneclawWalletProvider wraps the OneclawEmbeddedWallet component.

compliance-ops.title: Audit and compliance
compliance-ops.summary: Query the tamper-evident audit log and understand how writes are hardened.

blocks.text: Every API access and relevant failure can be recorded in an audit log. Only metadata is stored: path, action, actor, timestamp, IP. Secret values are never logged.

items: GET /v1/audit/events with filters: resource_id, actor_id, action, from, to, limit, offset
Each event carries prev_event_id and integrity_hash forming a SHA-256 hash chain
Modifying or deleting any event breaks the chain for every later entry
The app runs as vault_app, which cannot INSERT into audit_events directly

blocks.text: Audit writes go through a SECURITY DEFINER function (insert_audit_event) owned by a privileged role. Even a compromised app connection cannot fabricate events or bypass the hash chain.

steps.text: Set your API key.
Query recent secret reads.
Notice the response includes metadata only, plus prev_event_id and integrity_hash for chain verification.
Filter by a single actor to review one agent's activity.
To forward events to your SIEM, register an AuditSink when constructing the TypeScript SDK client. Every event your session produces is mirrored to your target.
steps.code: // see the SDK overview for the AuditSink plugin interface

blocks.text: Verify integrity by walking the chain from the latest event back to the first, where prev_event_id is NULL. Any break flags tampering.
You can now query the audit log by action and actor and explain why the chain plus SECURITY DEFINER insert makes it tamper-evident, which supports SOC 2 controls like CC6 and CC7.

quiz.question: What is stored in an audit event?
options: The secret value and the metadata
Only metadata such as path, action, actor, and timestamp
quiz.explanation: Audit events store metadata only; secret values are never logged.

quiz.question: How does the log resist tampering at write time?
options: Writes go through a SECURITY DEFINER function since vault_app cannot INSERT directly
Events are encrypted with the org KEK
quiz.explanation: vault_app has direct INSERT revoked, so all writes funnel through the trusted insert_audit_event function that enforces the chain.

quiz.question: What links each event to the previous one?
quiz.explanation: Each event carries prev_event_id and an integrity_hash, forming an append-only SHA-256 chain.

compliance-ops.title: Agent fleet management
compliance-ops.summary: Provision agents at scale, scope their vaults, then rotate a key and deactivate an agent.

blocks.text: When you run dozens or hundreds of agents you need repeatable patterns for enrollment, vault layout, and lifecycle. Bulk provisioning plus tight policies keeps the blast radius small.

items: Shared vault with path-scoped policies: api-keys/** shared, agents/{name}/** per-agent
Per-agent vaults for strict isolation in multi-tenant or compliance setups
Vault binding via vault_ids limits which vaults an agent JWT can touch even if a policy is too broad
Conditional policies add ip_allowlist and time_window on sensitive paths

steps.text: Set a human API key.
Bulk provision agents with the CLI.
steps.code:
for name in worker-1 worker-2 worker-3; do
  1claw agent create "$name" --scopes "vaults:read"
done

steps.text: Bind each agent to just its intended vaults so a stray policy cannot widen access.
Set transaction guardrails for any agent using the Intents API.
Rotate an agent's key. This invalidates the old credential.
Deactivate the agent when its task is done. This keeps the audit trail, unlike delete.

blocks.text: Prefer deactivate over delete. A deactivated agent cannot exchange tokens or read secrets, but its records and audit events are preserved for review.
You now rotate a key and deactivate an agent while keeping its audit trail, and you can scope a fleet with vault binding and conditional policies.

quiz.question: What does vault binding (vault_ids) add on top of policies?
options: It grants access to every vault
It restricts which vaults the agent JWT works for regardless of policies
quiz.explanation: Vault binding is a second layer: even a too-broad policy cannot let the JWT reach vaults outside vault_ids.

quiz.question: Why deactivate an agent instead of deleting it?
options: Deactivate preserves the records and audit trail
quiz.explanation: Deactivating with --active false blocks access but preserves records and audit events; delete removes them.

quiz.question: For a common set of secrets shared by all fleet agents, what layout is recommended?
options: A shared vault with path-scoped policies
A single wildcard policy on production
quiz.explanation: A shared vault with path-based policies (api-keys/** shared, agents/{name}/** per-agent) scales cleanly.

compliance-ops.title: Platform API multitenancy
compliance-ops.summary: Register a platform app and bootstrap a user's vault, agent, and signing keys from a template.

blocks.text: The Platform API lets you build multi-tenant products on 1Claw. You register an app, define a bootstrap template, provision users, and hand each user a claim URL, all without your platform being able to read their secrets.
The Platform API requires a Pro or higher plan. Save the returned plt_ key when you create the app; it is not shown again.

items: Templates create a vault, agents, and policies in one atomic operation
In templates use nested intents: { enabled: true }, not the flat intents_api_enabled
signing_keys in the template are provisioned server-side; the plt_ key cannot read them across the org boundary
Bootstrap returns claim_url plus a one-time agent_api_key in summary

steps.text: Register a platform app with your user JWT and save the plt_ key.
steps.code:
curl -X POST "https://api.1claw.xyz/v1/platform/apps" \
  -H "Authorization: Bearer $USER_JWT" -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{ "name": "My DeFi Platform", "slug": "my-defi", "auth_mode": "silent" }'

steps.text: Create a bootstrap template. Note intents is a nested object here.
Upsert a user.
Bootstrap the user from the template. The response has claim_url and a one-time summary.agent_api_key.
Share the claim_url with the user. It expires in 10 minutes; reissue with the reissue-claim endpoint if it lapses.

blocks.text: For Sign in with 1Claw, the OAuth client_id must be your app slug (for example my-defi), not the app UUID. Passing the UUID returns Unknown client_id.
You now register a platform app and bootstrap a user with a vault, an intents-enabled agent, and server-side signing keys your platform cannot read, plus a claim URL to hand off.

quiz.question: In a bootstrap template, how do you enable the Intents API on an agent?
quiz.explanation: Templates use the nested intents: { enabled: true }; the flat intents_api_enabled is the direct agent-creation form.

quiz.question: What must client_id be for Sign in with 1Claw?
quiz.explanation: client_id is the app slug; passing the UUID returns Unknown client_id.

quiz.question: Why can't the platform read a bootstrapped user's signing keys?
options: The plt_ key cannot read signing keys across the org boundary
quiz.explanation: Signing keys are provisioned server-side and the plt_ key cannot read them across the org boundary, keeping custody separated.
